title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: bpgmm expects numeric matrix variables rows observations columns. Many R data sets use opposite convention, transpose selecting numeric variables. package convention X=[x11⋯x1n⋮⋮xp1⋯xpn], X = \begin{bmatrix} x_{11} & \cdots & x_{1n} \\ \vdots &        & \vdots \\ x_{p1} & \cdots & x_{pn} \end{bmatrix}, row jj variable jj column ii observation xix_i. Rows now correspond variables columns correspond observations.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  iris_numeric <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) iris_labels <- as.integer(iris$Species)  dim(iris_numeric) #> [1] 150   4  X <- t(iris_numeric) dim(X) #> [1]   4 150 rownames(X) #> [1] "Sepal.Length" "Sepal.Width"  "Petal.Length" "Petal.Width" X[, 1:3] #>              [,1] [,2] [,3] #> Sepal.Length  5.1  4.9  4.7 #> Sepal.Width   3.5  3.0  3.2 #> Petal.Length  1.4  1.4  1.3 #> Petal.Width   0.2  0.2  0.2

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: sampler requires finite numeric values. Handle missing values fitting. Common choices include complete-case filtering, domain-specific imputation, fitting model subset variables reliable measurements.
code: all(is.finite(X)) #> [1] TRUE storage.mode(X) #> [1] "double"

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: Mixture models sensitive measurement scale. variables measured different units, standardizing variable usually sensible default. variable jj, usual transformation xjiscaled=xji−x‾j⋅sj,sj2=1n−1∑=1n(xji−x‾j⋅)2. x_{ji}^{\mathrm{scaled}} = \frac{x_{ji} - \bar{x}_{j\cdot}}{s_j}, \qquad s_j^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1}\sum_{=1}^n (x_{ji} - \bar{x}_{j\cdot})^2 . package scale internally scientific applications need original measurement scale. Scaling explicit analysis choice.
code: X_scaled <- t(scale(t(X)))  round(rowMeans(X_scaled), 6) #> Sepal.Length  Sepal.Width Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>            0            0            0            0 round(apply(X_scaled, 1, sd), 6) #> Sepal.Length  Sepal.Width Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>            1            1            1            1

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: q_new latent-factor dimension assigned newly proposed clusters. controls dimension factor-analyzer part covariance model. paper’s notation, Λk∈ℝp×qk,yki∈ℝqk. \Lambda_k \\mathbb{R}^{p \times q_k}, \qquad y_{ki} \\mathbb{R}^{q_k}. package uses q_new qkq_k value newly created component. Useful starting points: q_new = 1 small examples covariance structure simple. q_new = 2 3 moderate-dimensional data. Larger values number variables observations support extra covariance flexibility. value smaller number observed variables.
code: p <- nrow(X_scaled) q_new <- min(2, p - 1) q_new #> [1] 2

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: m_range allowed cluster-number range. wide range gives RJMCMC freedom, also increases model space. Start scientifically reasonable range, assess sensitivity. data known reference label, iris, reference partition gives simple check range. unsupervised applications, use domain knowledge exploratory plots.
code: m_init <- 3 m_range <- c(1, 5) species_cols <- c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#009E73") plot(   X_scaled[1, ], X_scaled[2, ],   col = species_cols[iris_labels],   pch = 19,   xlab = rownames(X_scaled)[1],   ylab = rownames(X_scaled)[2],   main = "Scaled iris data",   asp = 1 ) legend(   "topleft",   legend = levels(iris$Species),   col = species_cols,   pch = 19,   bty = "n" )

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: three-letter model labels describe whether loading matrices noise covariances shared across clusters. UUU flexible; CCC constrained. flexible starting model often reasonable using v_step = 1, sampler can move across covariance structures. fitted covariance always Σk=ΛkΛk⊤+Ψk, \Sigma_k = \Lambda_k\Lambda_k^\top + \Psi_k, label controls whether Λk\Lambda_k Ψk\Psi_k shared whether Ψk\Psi_k isotropic diagonal.
code: model_to_constraint("UUU") #> [1] 0 0 0 constraint_to_model(c(1, 1, 1)) #> [1] "CCC"

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: call evaluated vignette applied analyses use longer chains repeated runs. records prepared objects enter package interface.
code: fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(   X = X_scaled,   m_init = m_init,   m_range = m_range,   q_new = q_new,   burn = 1000,   niter = 5000,   constraint = "UUU",   m_step = 1,   v_step = 1,   split_combine = 1,   verbose = FALSE )  summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = iris_labels)

title: Preparing data and choosing sampler settings
text: fitting: verify X numeric variables rows; remove impute missing non-finite values; decide whether variables scaled; choose scientifically plausible m_range; choose q_new smaller number variables; use multiple chains applied analyses.

title: Worked examples
text: pgmm_rjmcmc() expects numeric matrix variables rows observations columns. code creates two well-separated clusters two dimensions.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  set.seed(2026)  X <- cbind(   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = -2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2),   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = 2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2) ) known_labels <- rep(1:2, each = 4)  dim(X) #> [1] 2 8 known_labels #> [1] 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 cluster_cols <- c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#009E73") plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[known_labels],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Simulated data",   asp = 1 ) legend(   "topleft",   legend = paste("Known", sort(unique(known_labels))),   col = cluster_cols[sort(unique(known_labels))],   pch = 19,   bty = "n" )

title: Worked examples
text: fit keeps mm covariance model fixed, output structure explicit. fitted likelihood contribution p(xi∣zi=k,Θ)=Np(xi∣μk,ΛkΛk⊤+Ψk). p(x_i \mid z_i = k, \Theta) = N_p(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Lambda_k\Lambda_k^\top + \Psi_k). model selection across mm covariance labels, use separate model-selection vignette. returned object stores posterior samples allocations, covariance constraints, component parameters, hyperparameters.
code: fit_log <- capture.output({   fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(     X = X,     m_init = 2,     m_range = c(1, 3),     q_new = 1,     burn = 1,     niter = 3,     constraint = "UUU",     m_step = 0,     v_step = 0,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) tail(fit_log, 1) #> character(0) names(fit) #>  [1] "tau_samples"            "psi_samples"            #>  [3] "mean_samples"           "lambda_samples"         #>  [5] "factor_score_samples"   "allocation_samples"     #>  [7] "constraint_samples"     "alpha1_samples"         #>  [9] "alpha2_samples"         "beta_samples"           #> [11] "active_cluster_samples" length(fit$allocation_samples) #> [1] 3

title: Worked examples
text: summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc() returns modal allocation, posterior counts number clusters, posterior counts covariance models, optionally adjusted Rand index. modal allocation computed coordinate-wise saved posterior allocations: ẑ=mode⁡{zi(1),…,zi(S)}, \hat{z}_i = \operatorname{mode}\{z_i^{(1)},\ldots,z_i^{(S)}\}, SS number saved posterior samples.
code: fit_summary <- summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = known_labels)  fit_summary$allocation #> [1] 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 fit_summary$n_clusters #>  #> 2  #> 3 fit_summary$n_constraints #>  #> UUU  #>   3 fit_summary$ari #> [1] 1 old_par <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[known_labels],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Known labels",   asp = 1 ) plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[fit_summary$allocation],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Posterior allocation",   asp = 1 ) par(old_par)

title: Worked examples
text: sampler stores draws τk\tau_k, μk\mu_k, Λk\Lambda_k, Ψk\Psi_k. fixed-model example, final saved draw enough show object shape.

what: Scale the example to real data
title: Worked examples
text: applied analysis, keep sequence steps use larger sampler settings. exact values depend data size convergence diagnostics.
code: fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(   X = your_data_matrix,   m_init = 2,   m_range = c(1, 10),   q_new = 4,   burn = 5000,   niter = 15000,   constraint = model_to_constraint("UUU"),   m_step = 1,   v_step = 1,   split_combine = 1,   verbose = FALSE )  summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = known_labels)

title: Worked examples
text: concise analysis report, include: data orientation preprocessing choices; m_range, q_new, starting covariance model, whether m_step, v_step, split_combine enabled; number burn-posterior iterations; posterior modal allocation co-clustering matrix; ARI reference partition scientifically meaningful. diagnostics vignette gives examples trace plots co-clustering summaries multiple chains.

title: Getting started with bpgmm
text: Input data numeric matrix variables rows observations columns. small example two variables eight observations. example short vignette runs quickly; applied analyses use larger burn niter values. scatter plot gives direct check simulated partition. point one observation, colors show reference labels used later ARI calculation.  call fixes m=2m = 2 covariance model example fast. important arguments : m_init initial number clusters. m_range allowed range number clusters. q_new number latent factors new cluster. constraint selects starting covariance model. m_step = 1 allows RJMCMC updates number clusters. v_step = 1 allows RJMCMC updates across covariance-constraint models.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  set.seed(2026)  X <- cbind(   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = -2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2),   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = 2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2) ) known_labels <- rep(1:2, each = 4) plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00")[known_labels],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Small two-cluster example",   asp = 1 ) legend(   "topleft",   legend = paste("Reference", sort(unique(known_labels))),   col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00"),   pch = 19,   bty = "n" ) fit_log <- capture.output({   fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(     X = X,     m_init = 2,     m_range = c(1, 3),     q_new = 1,     burn = 1,     niter = 3,     constraint = model_to_constraint("UUU"),     m_step = 0,     v_step = 0,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) tail(fit_log, 1) #> character(0)

title: Getting started with bpgmm
text: reference partition available, pass true_cluster calculate adjusted Rand index. summary allocation can plotted back original coordinates. plot first check longer chains convergence diagnostics.
code: summary <- summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = known_labels)  summary$allocation #> [1] 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 summary$n_clusters #>  #> 2  #> 3 summary$n_constraints #>  #> UUU  #>   3 summary$ari #> [1] 1 plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = c("#009E73", "#CC79A7", "#E69F00")[summary$allocation],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Posterior modal allocation",   asp = 1 ) text(X[1, ], X[2, ], labels = seq_along(summary$allocation), pos = 3, cex = 0.75) legend(   "topleft",   legend = paste("Cluster", sort(unique(summary$allocation))),   col = c("#009E73", "#CC79A7", "#E69F00")[sort(unique(summary$allocation))],   pch = 19,   bty = "n" )

title: Getting started with bpgmm
text: Common early problems : X accidentally supplied observations variables instead variables observations; variables different measurement scales standardized; m_init outside m_range; q_new large small number variables; m_step v_step left zero model selection desired.

title: Getting started with bpgmm
text: Starting version 1.2.0, public API uses snake_case names throughout. Use pgmm_rjmcmc(), summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(), snake_case argument names m_init, m_range, q_new.

title: Getting started with bpgmm
text: use bpgmm published work, please cite package methodology paper: Lu, X., Li, Y., & Love, T. (2021). Bayesian Analysis Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal Classification, 38, 576-593. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8
code: citation("bpgmm") #> To cite package 'bpgmm' in publications use: #>  #>   Lu X, Li Y, Love T (2021). On Bayesian Analysis of #>   Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal of #>   Classification, 38, 576-593. doi:10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8 #>  #> A BibTeX entry for LaTeX users is #>  #>   @Article{, #>     title = {On Bayesian Analysis of Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models}, #>     author = {Xiang Lu and Yaoxiang Li and Tanzy Love}, #>     journal = {Journal of Classification}, #>     year = {2021}, #>     volume = {38}, #>     pages = {576--593}, #>     doi = {10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8}, #>   }

title: Model and sampler details
text: Let X=(x1,…,xn)X = (x_1, \ldots, x_n) matrix continuous observations pp variables nn observations. bpgmm expects orientation: variables rows observations columns. cluster kk, mixture factor analyzers model writes xi=μk+Λkyki+ϵki, x_i = \mu_k + \Lambda_k y_{ki} + \epsilon_{ki}, yki∼N(0,Iqk),ϵki∼N(0,Ψk). y_{ki} \sim N(0, I_{q_k}), \qquad \epsilon_{ki} \sim N(0, \Psi_k). integrating latent factor ykiy_{ki}, cluster-specific covariance Σk=ΛkΛk⊤+Ψk. \Sigma_k = \Lambda_k \Lambda_k^\top + \Psi_k. plot shows geometric role covariance. loading matrix Λk\Lambda_k controls dominant low-dimensional direction, Ψk\Psi_k adds cluster-specific noise around subspace.  mixture density f(xi)=∑k=1mτkN(xi∣μk,ΛkΛk⊤+Ψk), f(x_i) = \sum_{k = 1}^m \tau_k   N(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Lambda_k \Lambda_k^\top + \Psi_k), τk\tau_k mixture weight mm number clusters. Equivalently, allocation indicator zi∈{1,…,m}z_i \\{1,\ldots,m\}, xi∣zi=k,Θ∼Np(μk,Σk),Pr⁡(zi=k∣τ)=τk. x_i \mid z_i = k, \Theta   \sim N_p(\mu_k, \Sigma_k), \qquad \Pr(z_i = k \mid \tau) = \tau_k . pgmm_rjmcmc(): X p×np \times n data matrix. m_init starting value mm. m_range allowed range mm. q_new factor dimension assigned newly proposed cluster. constraint starting covariance model.
code: ellipse_points <- function(center, sigma, level = 0.90, n = 120) {   eig <- eigen(sigma, symmetric = TRUE)   angles <- seq(0, 2 * pi, length.out = n)   circle <- rbind(cos(angles), sin(angles))   radius <- sqrt(qchisq(level, df = 2))   points <- t(center + radius * eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(eig$values), 2) %*% circle)   colnames(points) <- c("x", "y")   points }  lambda_1 <- matrix(c(1.1, 0.4), ncol = 1) lambda_2 <- matrix(c(0.2, 1.0), ncol = 1) psi_1 <- diag(c(0.20, 0.08)) psi_2 <- diag(c(0.10, 0.25)) sigma_1 <- lambda_1 %*% t(lambda_1) + psi_1 sigma_2 <- lambda_2 %*% t(lambda_2) + psi_2  ell_1 <- ellipse_points(c(-1.2, -0.5), sigma_1) ell_2 <- ellipse_points(c(1.1, 0.6), sigma_2)  plot(   ell_1,   type = "l",   lwd = 2,   col = "#0072B2",   xlim = c(-3.5, 3.5),   ylim = c(-2.5, 3),   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Mixture-of-factor-analyzers covariance" ) lines(ell_2, lwd = 2, col = "#D55E00") points(rbind(c(-1.2, -0.5), c(1.1, 0.6)), pch = 19, col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00")) arrows(-1.2, -0.5, -1.2 + lambda_1[1], -0.5 + lambda_1[2], col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2, length = 0.08) arrows(1.1, 0.6, 1.1 + lambda_2[1], 0.6 + lambda_2[2], col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2, length = 0.08) legend(   "topleft",   legend = c("Cluster 1 covariance", "Cluster 2 covariance", "Loading direction"),   col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "gray30"),   lwd = c(2, 2, 2),   bty = "n" )

title: Model and sampler details
text: paper uses three letters describe covariance structure. letter either C constrained U unconstrained. eight PGMM models : package uses numeric encoding internally: 1 means constrained 0 means unconstrained.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  models <- c("CCC", "CCU", "CUC", "CUU", "UCC", "UCU", "UUC", "UUU") data.frame(   model = models,   constraint = vapply(     models,     function(x) paste(model_to_constraint(x), collapse = ","),     character(1)   ) ) #>     model constraint #> CCC   CCC      1,1,1 #> CCU   CCU      1,1,0 #> CUC   CUC      1,0,1 #> CUU   CUU      1,0,0 #> UCC   UCC      0,1,1 #> UCU   UCU      0,1,0 #> UUC   UUC      0,0,1 #> UUU   UUU      0,0,0 model_to_constraint("UUU") #> [1] 0 0 0 constraint_to_model(c(1, 1, 0)) #> [1] "CCU"

title: Model and sampler details
text: supplement gives natural conjugate priors used MCMC updates. main priors : τ∼Dirichlet(γ,…,γ), \tau \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\gamma, \ldots, \gamma), yki∼N(0,Iqk), y_{ki} \sim N(0, I_{q_k}), μk∼N(x‾,α1−1Ψk), \mu_k \sim N(\bar{x}, \alpha_1^{-1} \Psi_k), λkj∼N(0,α2−1Ψk), \lambda_{kj} \sim N(0, \alpha_2^{-1} \Psi_k), ψkj∼IG(δ,β). \psi_{kj} \sim \mathrm{IG}(\delta, \beta). complete parameter state can read Θ={τk,μk,Λk,Ψk,yki,zi:k=1,…,m;=1,…,n}, \Theta = \{\tau_k, \mu_k, \Lambda_k, \Psi_k, y_{ki}, z_i:   k = 1,\ldots,m;\ = 1,\ldots,n\}, hyperparameters H=(α1,α2,β,δ,γ). H = (\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \beta, \delta, \gamma). package samples hyperparameters α1\alpha_1, α2\alpha_2, β\beta, gamma hyperpriors controlled : d_vec: shape parameters. s_vec: rate parameters. delta: inverse-gamma shape noise covariance. ggamma: Dirichlet concentration mixture weights. allocation update uses posterior probability pki=τkN(xi∣μk,Ψk+ΛkΛk⊤)∑ℓ=1mτℓN(xi∣μℓ,Ψℓ+ΛℓΛℓ⊤). p_{ki} = \frac{   \tau_k N(x_i \mid \mu_k, \Psi_k + \Lambda_k\Lambda_k^\top) }{   \sum_{\ell = 1}^{m}   \tau_\ell N(x_i \mid \mu_\ell, \Psi_\ell + \Lambda_\ell\Lambda_\ell^\top) }. (zi1,…,zim)∣X,Θ,H∼Multinomial(1,p1i,…,pmi). (z_{i1}, \ldots, z_{im}) \mid X, \Theta, H   \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(1, p_{1i}, \ldots, p_{mi}). allocation update uses probability, joint posterior includes product allocated mixture weights: p(Z∣τ)=∏=1nτzi. p(Z \mid \tau) = \prod_{= 1}^{n} \tau_{z_i}.

title: Model and sampler details
text: RJMCMC used number parameters changes number clusters covariance constraint changes. cluster-number moves : covariance-structure moves toggle one constraint time: toggle whether Λk\Lambda_k shared across clusters; toggle whether Ψk\Psi_k shared across clusters; toggle whether Ψk\Psi_k isotropic diagonal. pgmm_rjmcmc(): m_step = 1 enables birth/death moves mm. split_combine = 1 adds split/combine moves m_step = 1. v_step = 1 enables covariance-constraint moves. proposed move model MM M′M', RJMCMC acceptance probability standard form =min⁡{1,p(X,Z,Θ′,H′∣M′)p(M′)q(M∣M′)p(X,Z,Θ,H∣M)p(M)q(M′∣M)|J|}, = \min\left\{ 1, \frac{   p(X, Z, \Theta', H' \mid M')\,p(M')\,q(M \mid M') }{   p(X, Z, \Theta, H \mid M)\,p(M)\,q(M' \mid M) } \left|J\right| \right\}, q(⋅)q(\cdot) proposal density |J||J| Jacobian term dimension-changing moves split/combine. Birth/death split/combine change mm; covariance moves change constraint label v∈{CCC,…,UUU}v \\{\mathrm{CCC}, \ldots, \mathrm{UUU}\}.

title: Model and sampler details
text: formula terms map package output fields follows: applied model-selection call usually following structure:
code: fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(   X = your_data_matrix,   m_init = 5,   m_range = c(1, 10),   q_new = 4,   burn = 5000,   niter = 15000,   constraint = model_to_constraint("UUU"),   m_step = 1,   v_step = 1,   split_combine = 1,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Model and sampler details
text: use methods, cite: Lu, X., Li, Y., & Love, T. (2021). Bayesian Analysis Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal Classification, 38, 576-593. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8 R:
code: citation("bpgmm") #> To cite package 'bpgmm' in publications use: #>  #>   Lu X, Li Y, Love T (2021). On Bayesian Analysis of #>   Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal of #>   Classification, 38, 576-593. doi:10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8 #>  #> A BibTeX entry for LaTeX users is #>  #>   @Article{, #>     title = {On Bayesian Analysis of Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models}, #>     author = {Xiang Lu and Yaoxiang Li and Tanzy Love}, #>     journal = {Journal of Classification}, #>     year = {2021}, #>     volume = {38}, #>     pages = {576--593}, #>     doi = {10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8}, #>   }

title: Model selection on a larger simulated MFA data set
text: simulator creates three clusters, six observed variables, two latent factors. first three variables carry cluster separation, last three variables intentionally less informative. generating model matches paper’s mixture--factor-analyzers form: xi=μzi+Λziyi+ϵi,yi∼N2(0,I2),ϵi∼N6(0,Ψzi). x_i = \mu_{z_i} + \Lambda_{z_i}y_i + \epsilon_i, \qquad y_i \sim N_2(0, I_2), \qquad \epsilon_i \sim N_6(0, \Psi_{z_i}). first two variables already show substantial separation, model fit six variables.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  simulate_mfa_data <- function(n_per_cluster = 20, p = 6, q = 2) {   means <- rbind(     c(-3.0, -2.0,  0.0, 0, 0, 0),     c( 2.5, -1.0,  2.0, 0, 0, 0),     c( 0.0,  2.5, -2.0, 0, 0, 0)   )    lambdas <- list(     matrix(c(1.2,  0.5, 0.2, 0, 0, 0,              0.0,  0.2, 0.8, 0.3, 0, 0), nrow = p),     matrix(c(0.2,  1.1, 0.6, 0, 0, 0,              0.8,  0.1, 0.2, 0.6, 0, 0), nrow = p),     matrix(c(0.7, -0.4, 1.0, 0, 0, 0,             -0.2,  0.8, 0.4, 0.3, 0, 0), nrow = p)   )    psi <- list(     diag(c(0.25, 0.35, 0.30, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00)),     diag(c(0.30, 0.25, 0.40, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00)),     diag(c(0.35, 0.30, 0.25, 0.80, 0.90, 1.00))   )    n <- n_per_cluster * 3   X <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = p, ncol = n)   true_cluster <- rep(seq_len(3), each = n_per_cluster)    column <- 1   for (k in seq_len(3)) {     for (i in seq_len(n_per_cluster)) {       latent_score <- rnorm(q)       noise <- MASS::mvrnorm(1, mu = rep(0, p), Sigma = psi[[k]])       X[, column] <- means[k, ] + lambdas[[k]] %*% latent_score + noise       column <- column + 1     }   }    rownames(X) <- paste0("variable_", seq_len(p))   list(X = X, true_cluster = true_cluster) }  set.seed(2027) sim <- simulate_mfa_data() X <- sim$X true_cluster <- sim$true_cluster  dim(X) #> [1]  6 60 table(true_cluster) #> true_cluster #>  1  2  3  #> 20 20 20 cluster_cols <- c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#009E73", "#CC79A7", "#E69F00") plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[true_cluster],   pch = 19,   xlab = rownames(X)[1],   ylab = rownames(X)[2],   main = "Simulated MFA data",   asp = 1 ) legend(   "topleft",   legend = paste("True cluster", sort(unique(true_cluster))),   col = cluster_cols[sort(unique(true_cluster))],   pch = 19,   bty = "n" )

title: Model selection on a larger simulated MFA data set
text: m_step = 1 allows sampler move across different numbers clusters, v_step = 1 allows moves across PGMM covariance structures. example uses iterations vignette fast. two discrete targets p(m∣X)andp(v∣X), p(m \mid X) \quad \text{} \quad p(v \mid X), vv one eight covariance labels {CCC,…,UUU}\{\mathrm{CCC}, \ldots, \mathrm{UUU}\}. saved samples estimate posterior probabilities empirical frequencies: p̂(m=r∣X)=1S∑s=1SI(m(s)=r),p̂(v=∣X)=1S∑s=1SI(v(s)=). \widehat{p}(m = r \mid X)   = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S (m^{(s)} = r), \qquad \widehat{p}(v = \mid X)   = \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^S (v^{(s)} = ). model-selection summaries posterior sample counts. applied analysis, bars based much longer chain.
code: fit_log <- capture.output({   fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(     X = X,     m_init = 3,     m_range = c(1, 5),     q_new = 2,     burn = 2,     niter = 6,     constraint = "UUU",     m_step = 1,     v_step = 1,     split_combine = 0,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) tail(fit_log, 1) #> character(0) fit_summary <- summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = true_cluster)  fit_summary$n_clusters #>  #> 3  #> 6 fit_summary$n_constraints #>  #> UCC UCU UUU  #>   4   1   1 fit_summary$ari #> [1] 1 old_par <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 4, 3, 1)) barplot(   fit_summary$n_clusters,   col = "#56B4E9",   border = NA,   xlab = "Number of clusters",   ylab = "Posterior sample count",   main = "Cluster-number selection" ) barplot(   fit_summary$n_constraints,   col = "#E69F00",   border = NA,   xlab = "PGMM model",   ylab = "Posterior sample count",   main = "Covariance-model selection" ) par(old_par)

title: Model selection on a larger simulated MFA data set
text: posterior modal allocation can plotted known simulation labels.
code: old_par <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[true_cluster],   pch = 19,   xlab = rownames(X)[1],   ylab = rownames(X)[2],   main = "True clusters",   asp = 1 ) plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[fit_summary$allocation],   pch = 19,   xlab = rownames(X)[1],   ylab = rownames(X)[2],   main = "Posterior modal allocation",   asp = 1 ) par(old_par)

title: Model selection on a larger simulated MFA data set
text: short chain vignette demonstrates mechanics model selection. interpreted final posterior analysis. applied work: increase burn niter summaries stable; compare independent chains pgmm_rjmcmc_chains(); check whether sampled cluster counts stuck either edge m_range; rerun sensitivity checks plausible values q_new; inspect whether covariance-model posterior counts change v_step = 1. posterior mass stays upper end m_range, expand range revisit preprocessing. stays lower end, data may support fewer clusters expected, covariance model may flexible sample size.

title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  set.seed(2029) X <- cbind(   matrix(rnorm(10, mean = -2.0, sd = 0.25), nrow = 2),   matrix(rnorm(10, mean =  0.0, sd = 0.25), nrow = 2),   matrix(rnorm(10, mean =  2.0, sd = 0.25), nrow = 2) ) known_labels <- rep(1:3, each = 5) cluster_cols <- c("#0072B2", "#D55E00", "#009E73", "#CC79A7") plot(   X[1, ], X[2, ],   col = cluster_cols[known_labels],   pch = 19,   xlab = "Variable 1",   ylab = "Variable 2",   main = "Diagnostic example",   asp = 1 )

title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
text: pgmm_rjmcmc_chains() runs independent chains. vignette uses cores = 1 CRAN portability, users can increase cores locally.
code: fits <- pgmm_rjmcmc_chains(   X = X,   m_init = 3,   m_range = c(1, 4),   q_new = 1,   burn = 1,   niter = 4,   constraint = "UUU",   m_step = 1,   v_step = 1,   chains = 2,   cores = 1,   seed = 2029,   verbose = FALSE )  length(fits) #> [1] 2 attr(fits, "chain_seeds") #> [1] 370199374 178884786

title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
text: chain cc, simple empirical summaries p̂c(m=r∣X)=1Sc∑s=1ScI(mc(s)=r),p̂c(v=∣X)=1Sc∑s=1ScI(vc(s)=). \widehat{p}_c(m = r \mid X)   = \frac{1}{S_c}\sum_{s=1}^{S_c} (m_c^{(s)} = r), \qquad \widehat{p}_c(v = \mid X)   = \frac{1}{S_c}\sum_{s=1}^{S_c} (v_c^{(s)} = ). Large disagreements across chains warning sign, especially chains use different random seeds.
code: chain_summaries <- lapply(fits, summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc, true_cluster = known_labels)  data.frame(   chain = names(chain_summaries),   ari = vapply(chain_summaries, function(x) x$ari, numeric(1)),   modal_clusters = vapply(chain_summaries, function(x) {     as.integer(names(which.max(x$n_clusters)))   }, integer(1)) ) #>           chain       ari modal_clusters #> chain_1 chain_1 0.6410256              3 #> chain_2 chain_2 1.0000000              3

what: Trace cluster counts and covariance models
title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
text: posterior samples store active cluster indicators covariance constraint saved iteration. traces first diagnostics.
code: cluster_count_trace <- function(fit) {   vapply(fit$active_cluster_samples, sum, numeric(1)) }  constraint_trace <- function(fit) {   vapply(fit$constraint_samples, constraint_to_model, character(1)) }  cluster_traces <- lapply(fits, cluster_count_trace) constraint_traces <- lapply(fits, constraint_trace)  cluster_traces #> $chain_1 #> [1] 3 3 3 3 #>  #> $chain_2 #> [1] 3 3 3 3 constraint_traces #> $chain_1 #> [1] "UCU" "UUU" "UUC" "UUU" #>  #> $chain_2 #> [1] "CUU" "CUU" "CUU" "CUU" old_par <- par(mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1)) plot(   cluster_traces[[1]],   type = "b",   pch = 19,   ylim = range(unlist(cluster_traces)),   col = "#0072B2",   xlab = "Saved iteration",   ylab = "Active clusters",   main = "Cluster-count trace" ) lines(cluster_traces[[2]], type = "b", pch = 19, col = "#D55E00") legend("topright", legend = names(fits), col = c("#0072B2", "#D55E00"), lty = 1, pch = 19, bty = "n") par(old_par)

title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
text: co-clustering matrix estimates often two observations assigned cluster across posterior samples. reports pairwise uncertainty rather single modal allocation. Cij=Pr⁡(zi=zj∣X)≈1S∑s=1SI{zi(s)=zj(s)}. C_{ij} = \Pr(z_i = z_j \mid X) \approx \frac{1}{S}\sum_{s=1}^{S} \{z_i^{(s)} = z_j^{(s)}\}.
code: co_clustering_matrix <- function(fit) {   n <- length(fit$allocation_samples[[1]])   out <- matrix(0, n, n)    for (allocation in fit$allocation_samples) {     out <- out + outer(allocation, allocation, "==")   }    out / length(fit$allocation_samples) }  co_mat <- co_clustering_matrix(fits[[1]]) round(co_mat[1:6, 1:6], 2) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5] [,6] #> [1,] 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.25 #> [2,] 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.25 #> [3,] 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.25 #> [4,] 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.25 #> [5,] 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 1.00 0.25 #> [6,] 0.25 0.25 0.25 0.25 0.25 1.00 image(   seq_len(nrow(co_mat)),   seq_len(ncol(co_mat)),   co_mat[nrow(co_mat):1, ],   col = hcl.colors(20, "YlGnBu", rev = TRUE),   xlab = "Observation",   ylab = "Observation",   main = "Posterior co-clustering" )

title: Posterior diagnostics and multiple chains
text: Warning signs include: cluster-count traces stay boundary m_range; different posterior summaries across independent chains; covariance-model traces never move v_step = 1; co-clustering matrices clear block structure; sensitivity scaling, q_new, m_range. diagnostics replace long MCMC analysis, give concise description posterior output.

what: Simulate data with informative and weak variables
title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
text: simulation differs model-selection example. aim recover mm, examine posterior output can post-processed rank variables. first three variables different cluster means, variables four five mostly covariance differences, variable six weak noise. fitted PGMM still uses Σk=ΛkΛk⊤+Ψk, \Sigma_k = \Lambda_k\Lambda_k^\top + \Psi_k, simulation deliberately separates mean-driven variables covariance-driven variables two rankings answer different questions.
code: library(bpgmm) #> bpgmm 1.3.3 loaded. If you use bpgmm in published work, please cite it with citation("bpgmm").  simulate_screening_data <- function(n_per_cluster = 18, p = 6) {   means <- rbind(     c(-2.5, -1.5,  0.0, 0, 0, 0),     c( 2.0, -0.5,  1.8, 0, 0, 0),     c( 0.0,  2.2, -1.8, 0, 0, 0)   )    covariances <- list(     diag(c(0.35, 0.35, 0.35, 1.40, 0.35, 1.20)),     diag(c(0.35, 0.35, 0.35, 0.35, 1.40, 1.20)),     matrix(c(       0.35, 0,    0,    0,    0,    0,       0,    0.35, 0,    0,    0,    0,       0,    0,    0.35, 0,    0,    0,       0,    0,    0,    1.00, 0.45, 0,       0,    0,    0,    0.45, 1.00, 0,       0,    0,    0,    0,    0,    1.20     ), nrow = p)   )    n <- n_per_cluster * 3   X <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = p, ncol = n)   true_cluster <- rep(seq_len(3), each = n_per_cluster)    column <- 1   for (k in seq_len(3)) {     for (i in seq_len(n_per_cluster)) {       X[, column] <- MASS::mvrnorm(1, mu = means[k, ], Sigma = covariances[[k]])       column <- column + 1     }   }    rownames(X) <- paste0("variable_", seq_len(p))   list(X = X, true_cluster = true_cluster) }  set.seed(2028) sim <- simulate_screening_data() X <- sim$X true_cluster <- sim$true_cluster

title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
code: fit_log <- capture.output({   fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(     X = X,     m_init = 3,     m_range = c(1, 5),     q_new = 2,     burn = 2,     niter = 6,     constraint = "UUU",     m_step = 1,     v_step = 1,     verbose = FALSE   ) }) tail(fit_log, 1) #> character(0)  fit_summary <- summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = true_cluster) fit_summary$ari #> [1] 1

what: Rank variables by posterior allocation separation
title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
text: One simple score fraction total variation explained posterior modal clusters: Rj2=∑knk(x‾jk−x‾j⋅)2∑(xji−x‾j⋅)2. R_j^2 = \frac{\sum_k n_k(\bar{x}_{jk} - \bar{x}_{j\cdot})^2}      {\sum_i (x_{ji} - \bar{x}_{j\cdot})^2}. quantity posterior inclusion probability; summarizes separation fitted partition.
code: cluster_separation <- function(X, allocation) {   vapply(seq_len(nrow(X)), function(j) {     x <- X[j, ]     overall <- mean(x)     total <- sum((x - overall)^2)     between <- sum(vapply(split(x, allocation), function(group) {       length(group) * (mean(group) - overall)^2     }, numeric(1)))     if (total == 0) 0 else between / total   }, numeric(1)) }  separation <- cluster_separation(X, fit_summary$allocation) separation_table <- data.frame(   variable = rownames(X),   separation = round(separation, 3) ) separation_table[order(separation_table$separation, decreasing = TRUE), ] #>     variable separation #> 1 variable_1      0.927 #> 3 variable_3      0.891 #> 2 variable_2      0.840 #> 5 variable_5      0.037 #> 6 variable_6      0.012 #> 4 variable_4      0.003 ordered <- order(separation, decreasing = TRUE) barplot(   separation[ordered],   names.arg = rownames(X)[ordered],   las = 2,   col = "#56B4E9",   border = NA,   ylab = "Between-cluster variation / total variation",   main = "Exploratory variable separation" )

title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
text: loading matrices describe observed variables relate latent factors inside component. simple posterior diagnostic average absolute loading magnitude across saved samples active components. Lj=1|𝒮|∑s∈𝒮1||∑k∈As1qk∑ℓ=1qk|λjkℓ(s)|, L_j = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{S}|} \sum_{s \\mathcal{S}} \frac{1}{|A_s|} \sum_{k \A_s} \frac{1}{q_k}\sum_{\ell = 1}^{q_k} |\lambda_{jk\ell}^{(s)}|, AsA_s set active clusters posterior sample ss. Large LjL_j means variable jj contributes strongly latent-factor covariance structure; posterior inclusion probability.
code: loading_importance <- function(fit) {   totals <- NULL   count <- 0    for (sample_id in seq_along(fit$lambda_samples)) {     lambdas <- fit$lambda_samples[[sample_id]]     active <- which(fit$active_cluster_samples[[sample_id]] == 1)      for (k in active) {       lambda <- lambdas[[k]]       if (!is.matrix(lambda)) {         next       }       score <- rowMeans(abs(lambda))       if (is.null(totals)) {         totals <- numeric(length(score))       }       totals <- totals + score       count <- count + 1     }   }    if (count == 0) {     return(rep(NA_real_, nrow(X)))   }   totals / count }  loading_score <- loading_importance(fit) loading_table <- data.frame(   variable = rownames(X),   mean_abs_loading = round(loading_score, 3) ) loading_table[order(loading_table$mean_abs_loading, decreasing = TRUE), ] #>     variable mean_abs_loading #> 5 variable_5            0.465 #> 4 variable_4            0.399 #> 6 variable_6            0.397 #> 3 variable_3            0.211 #> 2 variable_2            0.082 #> 1 variable_1            0.071 ordered_loading <- order(loading_score, decreasing = TRUE) barplot(   loading_score[ordered_loading],   names.arg = rownames(X)[ordered_loading],   las = 2,   col = "#E69F00",   border = NA,   ylab = "Mean absolute loading",   main = "Posterior loading magnitude" )

title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
text: Use summaries together: high separation means variable distinguishes posterior clusters; high loading magnitude means variable contributes latent-factor covariance structure inside components; disagreement two summaries indicates mean structure covariance structure carry different information. formal variable selection, model need explicit variable inclusion prior posterior inclusion summaries. current package best described supporting model-based clustering, cluster-number selection, PGMM covariance-structure selection, exploratory variable prioritization available posterior outputs.

title: Exploratory variable prioritization after bpgmm clustering
text: using summaries manuscript analysis report, use wording : ranked variables separation across posterior modal clusters posterior loading magnitudes. rankings exploratory diagnostics derived fitted clustering model, posterior inclusion probabilities. Avoid wording “selected variables” unless formal variable-selection model fitted. distinction keeps statistical target clear avoids overstating model.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Yaoxiang Li. Author, maintainer. Xiang Lu. Author. Tanzy Love. Author.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Lu X, Li Y, Love T (2021). Bayesian Analysis Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal Classification, 38, 576-593. doi:10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8
code: @Article{,   title = {On Bayesian Analysis of Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models},   author = {Xiang Lu and Yaoxiang Li and Tanzy Love},   journal = {Journal of Classification},   year = {2021},   volume = {38},   pages = {576--593},   doi = {10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8}, }

what: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: bpgmm implements Bayesian inference parsimonious Gaussian mixture models. used model-based clustering number clusters, object partition, cluster covariance structure inferential targets. package uses Markov chain Monte Carlo posterior estimation reversible-jump MCMC (RJMCMC) model selection across constrained mixtures factor analyzers.

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: Install released version CRAN: Load package:
code: install.packages("bpgmm") library(bpgmm)

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: package supports analyses inferential targets include: estimate number clusters data; infer posterior cluster membership probabilities; compare parsimonious covariance structures; fit mixture--factor-analyzers models high-dimensional clustering; use RJMCMC move across models different parameter dimensions.

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: example creates two small clusters, fits short RJMCMC chain, summarizes posterior samples. Applied analyses use longer burn-posterior samples. call, X numeric matrix variables rows observations columns. Set m_step = 1 allow RJMCMC updates number clusters v_step = 1 allow updates variance structure. main user-facing function pgmm_rjmcmc(). Important settings include: m_init: initial number clusters. m_range: allowed cluster range, c(1, 6). q_new: number latent factors newly proposed cluster. burn niter: burn-posterior sampling iterations. constraint: initial covariance model, usually set model_to_constraint(). m_step, v_step, split_combine: switches cluster-number, covariance-model, split/combine RJMCMC moves. verbose: set FALSE suppress per-iteration progress output.
code: set.seed(2026)  X <- cbind(   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = -2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2),   matrix(rnorm(8, mean = 2, sd = 0.2), nrow = 2) ) known_labels <- rep(1:2, each = 4)  fit <- pgmm_rjmcmc(   X = X,   m_init = 2,   m_range = c(1, 3),   q_new = 1,   burn = 1,   niter = 3,   constraint = model_to_constraint("UUU"),   m_step = 0,   v_step = 0,   verbose = FALSE )  fit_summary <- summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = known_labels) as.integer(fit_summary$n_clusters["2"]) #> [1] 3  as.integer(fit_summary$n_constraints["UUU"]) #> [1] 3  fit_summary$ari #> [1] 1

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: Individual RJMCMC iterations sequential state depends previous state. use multiple cores safely, run independent chains parallel: uses separate worker processes available stores deterministic per-chain seeds attr(fits, "chain_seeds"). Starting version 1.2.0, public API uses snake_case names throughout. Older camelCase function names argument names longer exported. eight covariance structures paper represented model labels "CCC", "CUU", "UUU". package also accepts legacy three-number constraint encoding:
code: fits <- pgmm_rjmcmc_chains(   X = X,   m_init = 2,   m_range = c(1, 3),   q_new = 1,   burn = 100,   niter = 1000,   chains = 4,   cores = 4,   seed = 2026,   verbose = FALSE )  length(fits) #> [1] 4 model_to_constraint("UUU") constraint_to_model(c(1, 0, 0))

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: methodology behind package described : Lu, X., Li, Y., & Love, T. (2021). Bayesian Analysis Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models. Journal Classification, 38, 576-593. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8 paper develops RJMCMC inferential procedure constrained mixture--factor-analyzers models. inferential goals partition observations, number clusters, covariance structure clusters, represented posterior distributions.

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: use bpgmm published work, please cite package methodology paper. R, run: BibTeX paper:
code: citation("bpgmm") @article{lu2021bayesian,   author = {Lu, Xiang and Li, Yaoxiang and Love, Tanzy},   title = {On Bayesian Analysis of Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models},   journal = {Journal of Classification},   year = {2021},   volume = {38},   pages = {576--593},   doi = {10.1007/s00357-021-09391-8} }

title: bpgmm: Bayesian inference for parsimonious Gaussian mixture models
text: bpgmm released GPL-3 license.

what: Hyperparameter set for the Bayesian PGMM sampler. — Hparam-class
title: Hyperparameter set for the Bayesian PGMM sampler. — Hparam-class
text: Hyperparameter set Bayesian PGMM sampler.

title: Hyperparameter set for the Bayesian PGMM sampler. — Hparam-class
text: alpha1 First Dirichlet prior parameter component weights. alpha2 Second Dirichlet prior parameter component weights. delta Shape parameter used prior updates. ggamma Prior rate parameter used covariance updates. bbeta Prior scale parameter used covariance updates.

title: ThetaYList-class — ThetaYList
text: Parameter set sampled PGMM component parameters.

title: ThetaYList-class — ThetaYList
text: tao Numeric vector component mixing weights. psy List diagonal noise covariance matrices. M List component mean vectors. lambda List component factor loading matrices. Y List latent factor score matrices.

what: Convert PGMM Constraint Codes to Paper Model Names — constraint_to_model
title: Convert PGMM Constraint Codes to Paper Model Names — constraint_to_model
text: paper represents eight PGMM covariance structures three-letter model names. letter either `C` constrained `U` unconstrained. letters indicate whether loading matrix shared across clusters, whether noise covariance shared across clusters, whether noise covariance isotropic within clusters.

title: Convert PGMM Constraint Codes to Paper Model Names — constraint_to_model
code: constraint_to_model(constraint)

title: Convert PGMM Constraint Codes to Paper Model Names — constraint_to_model
text: constraint Integer numeric vector length three entries `0` `1`. `1` maps `C`; `0` maps `U`.

title: Convert PGMM Constraint Codes to Paper Model Names — constraint_to_model
text: character scalar, one `CCC`, `CCU`, `CUC`, `CUU`, `UCC`,   `UCU`, `UUC`, `UUU`.

what: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names to Constraint Codes — model_to_constraint
title: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names to Constraint Codes — model_to_constraint
text: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names Constraint Codes

title: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names to Constraint Codes — model_to_constraint
code: model_to_constraint(model)

title: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names to Constraint Codes — model_to_constraint
text: model Character scalar naming one eight PGMM covariance structures: `CCC`, `CCU`, `CUC`, `CUU`, `UCC`, `UCU`, `UUC`, `UUU`.

title: Convert PGMM Paper Model Names to Constraint Codes — model_to_constraint
text: Integer vector length three. `1` means constrained `0` means   unconstrained.

what: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
title: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
text: Carries model-based clustering using parsimonious Gaussian mixture models. MCMC used parameter estimation RJMCMC used model selection.

title: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
code: pgmm_rjmcmc(   X,   m_init,   m_range,   q_new,   delta = 2,   ggamma = 2,   burn = 20,   niter = 1000,   constraint = c(0, 0, 0),   d_vec = c(1, 1, 1),   s_vec = c(1, 1, 1),   m_step = 0,   v_step = 0,   split_combine = 0,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
text: X observation matrix variables rows observations columns. m_init number initial clusters. m_range allowed range number clusters. q_new number latent factors new cluster. delta scalar hyperparameter noise covariance prior ggamma scalar hyperparameter used covariance-structure proposals burn number burn-iterations niter number posterior sampling iterations constraint initial PGMM covariance constraint. Use three-letter model label `"CCC"` `"UUU"`, numeric vector length three binary entries. example, `c(1, 1, 1)` `CCC`, fully constrained model, `c(0, 0, 0)` `UUU`, fully unconstrained model. d_vec vector hyperparameters length three, shape parameters alpha1, alpha2 bbeta respectively s_vec vector hyperparameters length three, rate parameters alpha1, alpha2 bbeta respectively m_step indicator RJMCMC model selection number clusters. v_step indicator RJMCMC model selection covariance structures. split_combine indicator using split/combine moves cluster-number RJMCMC step. verbose logical; `TRUE`, print iteration progress.

title: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
text: list posterior samples snake_case fields:   `tau_samples`, `psi_samples`, `mean_samples`, `lambda_samples`,   `factor_score_samples`, `allocation_samples`, `constraint_samples`,   `alpha1_samples`, `alpha2_samples`, `beta_samples`,   `active_cluster_samples`.

title: Bayesian Model-Based Clustering with Parsimonious Gaussian Mixture Models — pgmm_rjmcmc
text: `constraint` argument follows three-letter PGMM model notation used Lu, Li, Love (2021). first entry indicates whether loading matrices shared across clusters, second whether noise covariance matrices shared across clusters, third whether noise covariance isotropic within cluster. Use [model_to_constraint()] convert model names `CCC`, `CCU`, `CUC`, `CUU`, `UCC`, `UCU`, `UUC`, `UUU` numeric vector used internally.

what: Run Multiple Independent Bayesian PGMM Chains — pgmm_rjmcmc_chains
title: Run Multiple Independent Bayesian PGMM Chains — pgmm_rjmcmc_chains
text: Runs independent `pgmm_rjmcmc()` chains, optionally parallel. safest way use multiple CPU cores MCMC iteration depends previous state, independent chains can evaluated concurrently.

title: Run Multiple Independent Bayesian PGMM Chains — pgmm_rjmcmc_chains
code: pgmm_rjmcmc_chains(   X,   m_init,   m_range,   q_new,   delta = 2,   ggamma = 2,   burn = 20,   niter = 1000,   constraint = c(0, 0, 0),   d_vec = c(1, 1, 1),   s_vec = c(1, 1, 1),   m_step = 0,   v_step = 0,   split_combine = 0,   verbose = FALSE,   chains = 2,   cores = min(chains, available_cores()),   seed = NULL )

title: Run Multiple Independent Bayesian PGMM Chains — pgmm_rjmcmc_chains
text: X observation matrix variables rows observations columns. m_init number initial clusters. m_range allowed range number clusters. q_new number latent factors new cluster. delta scalar hyperparameter noise covariance prior ggamma scalar hyperparameter used covariance-structure proposals burn number burn-iterations niter number posterior sampling iterations constraint initial PGMM covariance constraint. Use three-letter model label `"CCC"` `"UUU"`, numeric vector length three binary entries. example, `c(1, 1, 1)` `CCC`, fully constrained model, `c(0, 0, 0)` `UUU`, fully unconstrained model. d_vec vector hyperparameters length three, shape parameters alpha1, alpha2 bbeta respectively s_vec vector hyperparameters length three, rate parameters alpha1, alpha2 bbeta respectively m_step indicator RJMCMC model selection number clusters. v_step indicator RJMCMC model selection covariance structures. split_combine indicator using split/combine moves cluster-number RJMCMC step. verbose logical; `TRUE`, print iteration progress. chains positive integer giving number independent chains. cores positive integer giving number worker processes use. Values greater `chains` reduced `chains`. seed optional integer seed used generate deterministic per-chain seeds.

title: Run Multiple Independent Bayesian PGMM Chains — pgmm_rjmcmc_chains
text: list one fitted `pgmm_rjmcmc()` result per chain. result   class `bpgmm_rjmcmc_chains` stores per-chain seeds   `chain_seeds` attribute.

what: Summarize RJMCMC Samples from a Bayesian PGMM Fit — summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc
title: Summarize RJMCMC Samples from a Bayesian PGMM Fit — summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc
text: Summarizes posterior samples [pgmm_rjmcmc()] modal allocation, posterior counts number clusters, posterior counts eight PGMM covariance-constraint models, optionally adjusted Rand index known reference partition.

title: Summarize RJMCMC Samples from a Bayesian PGMM Fit — summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc
code: summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc(fit, true_cluster = NULL)

title: Summarize RJMCMC Samples from a Bayesian PGMM Fit — summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc
text: fit Result list [pgmm_rjmcmc()]. true_cluster Optional true reference cluster allocation.

title: Summarize RJMCMC Samples from a Bayesian PGMM Fit — summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc
text: list `allocation`, `n_clusters`, `n_constraints`, optionally   `ari`.

title: bpgmm 1.3.3
text: Made cluster-mean prior Gibbs update consistent. pgmm_rjmcmc() now centers data internally cluster-mean prior mean xbar 0 (value implied augmented loading posterior Lu, Li, Love (2021, Supplement .1)), matching zero-mean conditional already used sampler. Previously prior mean set single random observation update used 0, two inconsistent. Sampled means returned original (uncentered) data scale. Corrected split/combine acceptance ratio. split proposes one independent sign per coordinate new component means, proposal density carries 2^-p factor (p = nrow(X)) evaluate_split_clusters() dropped. factor required split/combine move reversible two merged components uniformly ordered across coordinates. corresponding deterministic split paper omits sign randomization; see package errata notes. Fixed error (q_vec entries must positive integers) aborted cluster-number RJMCMC (m_step = 1, especially split_combine = 1) whenever active clusters became non-contiguous, example interior empty cluster removed. prior evaluators now compact active clusters, native latent-score update validates active clusters, combine evaluator uses merged cluster’s factor count.

title: bpgmm 1.3.2
text: Fixed beta hyperparameter Gibbs update Gamma rate uses rate hyperprior s_vec[3] (s_beta) instead shape hyperprior d_vec[3], matching conditional posterior Lu, Li, Love (2021, Appendix .1.3). Results unchanged default symmetric hyperpriors d_vec = s_vec = c(1, 1, 1) corrected user-supplied asymmetric beta hyperpriors. Fixed native latent-score update unallocated observations draw N(0, I_q) specified paper’s conditional scores, rather component score covariance. scores used downstream sufficient statistic, posterior results unaffected.

title: bpgmm 1.3.1
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-28 Revised vignette set reduce repeated examples across vignettes. Made model--sampler, data-preparation, model-selection, variable-prioritization, diagnostics vignettes formula-focused closer notation Lu, Li, Love (2021). Changed variable-prioritization simulation longer duplicates larger MFA model-selection example.

title: bpgmm 1.3.0
text: Standardized native Rcpp interface snake_case function names. Renamed C++ source files snake_case removed stale compiled artifacts src/. Cleaned internal native wrapper documentation preserving sampler behavior.

title: bpgmm 1.2.9
text: Added data-preparation posterior-diagnostics vignettes. Expanded vignette collection vignette covers distinct analysis task: input preparation, sampler details, small worked examples, larger model selection, exploratory variable prioritization, multi-chain diagnostics.

title: bpgmm 1.2.8
text: Added larger model-selection exploratory variable-prioritization vignettes runnable examples plots. Documented use posterior allocations loading matrices exploratory variable prioritization without presenting formal Bayesian variable selection.

title: bpgmm 1.2.7
text: Moved latent-factor score updates R loops native C++. Moved prior density evaluation lambda psi native C++ preserving existing internal R API. Added strict native tests latent-score sampling, input validation, closed-form prior density checks.

title: bpgmm 1.2.6
text: Added pgmm_rjmcmc_chains() CRAN-safe multi-core execution independent RJMCMC chains. Added deterministic per-chain seeding unit tests independent-chain wrapper.

title: bpgmm 1.2.5
text: Cleaned native C++ readability without changing sampler behavior. Replaced stale include guards prototypes, removed selected namespace pollution, clarified covariance-constraint branch checks model-label comments. Regenerated Rcpp exports tightening native helper signatures.

title: bpgmm 1.2.4
text: Optimized native allocation sampler precomputing component covariance factorizations sampling normalized log probabilities directly. Removed avoidable dense probability/log-density matrices repeated per-observation covariance validation update_PostZ(). Tightened native helper signatures avoid unnecessary copies removed stale debug-C++ comments posterior lambda/psi update.

title: bpgmm 1.2.3
text: Added CRAN-safe base R plots getting-started, worked-examples, model--sampler vignettes. Visualized simulated clusters, posterior modal allocations, posterior model counts, mixture--factor-analyzers covariance geometry.

title: bpgmm 1.2.2
text: Optimized native Calculate_Cxy() helper accumulating cluster sufficient statistics directly labels instead building dense allocation matrix multiplying zero weights. Removed repeated temporary vector construction inside Cxy accumulation loop. Preserved existing C++11 baseline CRAN portability.

title: bpgmm 1.2.1
text: Hardened Rcpp entry points explicit validation dimensions, finite numeric inputs, covariance constraints, native hyperparameter vectors. Kept native build settings existing CRAN-friendly C++11 baseline modernizing validation headers. Cleaned native headers removed stale C++ declarations debug-includes/comments. Expanded native unit tests invalid Cxy, posterior lambda/psi, hyperparameter-update inputs.

title: bpgmm 1.2.0
text: Made public API consistently snake_case now package CRAN reverse dependencies. Removed legacy exported wrappers pgmmRJMCMC(), summarizePgmmRJMCMC(), summerizePgmmRJMCMC(). Renamed public sampler arguments camelCase snake_case: m_init, m_range, q_new, d_vec, s_vec, m_step, v_step, split_combine. Renamed summary arguments result fields snake_case, including true_cluster, allocation, n_clusters, n_constraints, allocation_samples, constraint_samples. Updated README, pkgdown vignettes, examples, tests breaking 1.2.0 API.

title: bpgmm 1.1.6
text: Added model--sampler vignette explains mixture--factor-analyzers likelihood, PGMM covariance constraints, conjugate priors, allocation update, RJMCMC moves, package output fields formulas runnable examples. Linked new mathematical vignette getting-started guide pkgdown vignette index.

title: bpgmm 1.1.5
text: Fixed allocation prior contribution RJMCMC acceptance calculations use log product allocated mixture weights, matching paper’s joint posterior.

title: bpgmm 1.1.4
text: Standardized internal R helper names snake_case preserving exported compatibility wrappers. Added snake_case wrappers around generated Rcpp entry points routed package internals wrappers. Cleaned source layout renaming R files lowercase hyphenated names removing ignored duplicate R/choosem/ tree. Kept result-list names legacy public arguments stable avoid breaking existing user code.

title: bpgmm 1.1.3
text: Fixed Mstep = 1 loading cluster-number proposal helpers package code rather leaving ignored nested R/ directory. Fixed split/combine RJMCMC moves passing X explicitly split-allocation helpers adding missing latent-factor update helper. Fixed C++ allocation update add log(tao) component log densities instead adding raw mixture weights. Added C++ input validation multivariate normal densities, log-ratio calculations, allocation dimensions, mixture weights. Expanded unit coverage eight PGMM covariance constraints, C++ helpers, cluster-number RJMCMC, covariance RJMCMC, split/combine moves.

title: bpgmm 1.1.2
text: Modernized README badges, installation guidance, paper citation, model-constraint helper examples. Added summarizePgmmRJMCMC() correctly spelled summary function retained summerizePgmmRJMCMC() backward compatibility. Added tidyverse-style pgmm_rjmcmc() summarize_pgmm_rjmcmc() preferred public API. Deprecated pgmmRJMCMC(), summarizePgmmRJMCMC(), summerizePgmmRJMCMC(); names remain available compatibility wrappers. Added helpers model_to_constraint() constraint_to_model() translating paper model labels legacy constraint vectors. Improved package startup citation guidance users publishing results bpgmm. Added unit tests public API, covariance-constraint mapping, summary helpers, native C++ wrappers. Fixed zero-iteration handling pgmm_rjmcmc() added validation sampler inputs summary result objects. Added verbose argument suppress per-iteration progress output examples, tests, scripted workflows. Updated C++ build settings validation current R/Rcpp best practices.
